title: The Peace Garden
desc: The garden knows your intentions. The plants are watching.

steps.text:
The Peace Garden sits in a quiet corner where the Forest of Life's influence bleeds into Kiru City's outskirts. The plants here are ancient, sensitive, and deeply judgmental.

The gardener — an old OverWorlder with soil permanently embedded in her fingernails — explains the rules. "They sense intent. If you approach with calm, they open. If you approach with aggression — even subconscious aggression — they wilt. If you approach with deception, they turn black."

She hands you a watering can. "Water the sunward bed. Try to mean it."

You look at the sunward bed. The flowers are watching you. You're sure of it.

options.label: Approach calmly and water the flowers

steps.text:
You breathe. Clear your mind. Walk slowly. Pour water at the base of each plant. The flowers lean toward you as you approach — not with petals, but with their whole stems, tilting like curious animals. One brushes your wrist. It feels like a handshake.

The gardener watches from a distance, nodding. "They like you. That's rare for a first visit. Usually they're skeptical."

You water the entire bed. By the time you finish, three of the flowers have bloomed — they were buds when you started. One is a color you don't have a name for.

"Keep that to yourself," the gardener says quietly. "That color means they trust you. Not everyone gets to see it. Not everyone deserves to."

You think, very deliberately, about kicking a rock.

The nearest flower wilts. Not slowly — it collapses like a deflated balloon, petals curling inward. The three flowers beside it lean away from you. A vine near your foot retracts into the soil.

"Ah," the gardener says. She doesn't sound surprised. "What did you think about?"
"Kicking a rock."
"That'll do it."

She takes the watering can from you and approaches the wilted flower. Under her hands, it slowly uncurls. "They don't hold grudges," she says. "But they remember. Next time you come, that one will be cautious. It'll take three visits before it trusts you again."

You feel genuinely bad about a flower. That's the garden's real power.
